title: Wet felted table runner
subtitle: A long flat panel in layered merino colours that rolls up for storage and lays flat for use
excerpt: Wet felt a table runner 100 x 30 cm in two or three complementary merino colours layered and blended at the edges. The colours are laid in bands and worked together at the joins so no hard boundary shows in the finished cloth. Finished pieces wash in cool water and dry flat in minutes.
sourceNotes: Flat-panel wet felting with colour layering is a standard intermediate technique in textile guilds and workshop instruction. Method follows the cross-hatch layering approach common to the genre.

content.text: The runner is made by the standard flat-panel method scaled up to 100 x 30 cm. Because felt shrinks roughly 20-30% during fulling, the pre-felt layout should be about 130 x 40 cm. Two or three roving colours laid in lengthways bands with deliberate
at the joins give the finished piece a natural ombre rather than a stripe.

items.name: Merino roving in two or three colours
Hot water (as hot as comfortable) and cold water
Bamboo rolling mat (at least 100 cm wide)
Felting net or old sheer curtain (at least 130 x 45 cm)
Pool noodle or large cardboard tube for rolling

content.text: Step 1: Lay the first colour layer

Lay the bamboo mat on a table. Pull roving into strips about 5 cm wide. Lay the first colour in horizontal bands across the full 130 cm length, covering the centre two-thirds of the 40 cm width. Make the layer even: hold sections to the light and fill any obvious thin spots. Turn 90 degrees and add a second layer perpendicular to the first, still in the first colour.

Add the second colour in bands along both edges, overlapping about 5 cm into the first colour at each boundary. At the overlap zone, tease the fibres from each colour apart and interleave them by hand so they mix. This blended zone will felt into a gradient. Repeat the two-direction layering in the second colour.

Place the felting net over the layout. Pour hot soapy water gently and evenly over the net so the roving saturates without moving. Press with flat palms through the net working from centre to edges. Once fully saturated, rub in circular strokes through the net for 10 minutes. Flip the entire piece (roving, net, and mat together), peel back the mat, and repeat on the reverse side.

Once the surface is beginning to hold together, roll the piece tightly around the pool noodle (with the net still in place) and roll back and forth on the mat for 40-50 passes. Unroll, turn 90 degrees, re-roll, and repeat. After each rolling session, unroll and check: the runner should be visibly shrinking and the surface firming. Continue until the felt passes the pinch test firmly.

Rinse in warm water, then plunge into cold to set the felt. Press out moisture in a towel. Lay flat on a clean dry surface and gently stretch to the final dimensions. Do not hang wet felt to dry: the weight of the water will stretch the piece unevenly. Allow to dry flat.

items.fix: Turn the piece 90 degrees after every two rolling sessions and check the shape each time before continuing.
items.cause: Uneven rolling pressure or the noodle was not centred during rolling
items.problem: The runner is narrower at one end than the other

items.fix: In the next piece, spend more time at the boundary zone before wetting, interleaving and mixing the fibres from both colours by hand.
items.cause: The blending zone was not interleaved thoroughly before wetting out
items.problem: A hard colour boundary rather than a gradient appears in the finished piece

items.fix: Keep the palm flat and apply even pressure through the net in smooth circles. Rough friction causes surface pilling.
items.cause: The net was rubbed rather than the palm pressed smoothly through it
items.problem: The surface has a rough, pilled texture
